Why the NBA WILL NEVER reduce the season from 82 games. Give me a reason why?. Reddit Post Body: There’s always debate about reducing the NBA regular season—“make it 56 games,” “every game would matter more,” “less injuries,” etc. It sounds great. But it’s functionally impossible. Here’s why: 1. The CBA Makes It Nearly Impossible The Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA) ties league revenue directly to player salaries through Basketball Related Income (BRI). Players are guaranteed around 50% of all BRI. So unless team revenues drop, players still expect that full 50%. Now here’s the math problem: • NBA season = 82 games • Proposal = 56 games (a 32% reduction in games) 2. Players Won’t Take a 32% Pay Cut Let’s say a player makes $20 million over 82 games. That’s about $243,902 per game. If we drop to 56 games, keeping the same per-game pay means the player still makes $13.66 million — a loss of $6.34 million. Multiply that across the league and you’re talking about billions in lost player compensation. Not happening. 3. Teams & Owners Won’t Eat That Either Teams generate revenue through tickets, concessions, sponsorships, and local TV contracts, all based on 82 games. Less games = less revenue unless prices go up. 4. Fans Won’t Pay More for Less To keep revenue constant while cutting 26 games, ticket prices would need to increase by ~46%. Here’s the math: 82 games of revenue ÷ 56 games = 1.464x per-game revenue required. Are fans going to pay $146 for a seat that used to be $100? Maybe in a few markets. But not league-wide. 5. Conclusion The math, the CBA, and economic incentives don’t line up. • Owners won’t pay players the same for fewer games. • Players won’t take 30-40% pay cuts. • Fans won’t pay 40-50% more per ticket. Until BRI is restructured, or some wild new revenue stream appears (hello, global streaming deals?), a reduced NBA season is just fantasy

So, is hand checking legal now?. Just finished watching OKC/DEN and I am left with one question, is hand checking legal now? I was not rooting for either team going in, was just stoked for this game because the series has been awesome. It was obvious to me that OKC fouled the ball handler on almost every possession. I thought maybe I didn’t understand the definition of hand checking so I looked it up, and “Hand checking in the NBA refers to a defensive tactic where a defender uses one or both hands to impede the movement of an offensive player” and that perfectly describes OKC on almost every DEN possession tonight. The game was unwatchable. I had to turn it off in the 4th quarter.

Who will be the last team to win the NBA?. For the most part, we can only speculate. Some teams are closer than others—Minnesota and Indiana, for example—given their chances this year and likely in the seasons to come. But looking beyond the next six years? That’s anyone’s guess. Ignoring specific players and teams, the odds of being the last championship-less franchise will only get tougher. Right now, it’s a 1-in-3 situation, but as the list narrows, the final team will face a daunting 1-in-30 scenario. If the league maintains its current format for the next century, there’s no guarantee every team will win a title. Expansion could shake things up—maybe two new franchises join the mix—but in our lifetimes, it’s unlikely we’ll see every team crowned. So the real question is… **who will be the last team standing?**

Time for a change in the NBA Schedule. 1. 70-Game Season Starting December 1 Start the season December 1. Make Christmas Day the official league launch. Cutting 12 games improves player health, game quality, and keeps stars on the floor. 2. Designated Viewing Nights, Like College Football Move away from daily games. Focus on high-impact nights: • Monday: Rivalry Night • Wednesday: National TV Matchups • Friday: West Coast After Dark • Saturday: All-Day Showcase • Sunday: Playoff Implications NBA should be able to flex for the week similar to the NFL for the Sunday night game. 3. MLK Day = Full-League Celebration All 30 teams play. All games during the day. Tie in civil rights tributes, historic uniforms, and local storytelling. 4. NBA Cup Week – Final Week of January Top 8 teams compete in a best-of-3 FIBA-style tournament overseas. New rules, global spotlight, full-week event. Owners benefit through international media rights, sponsors, and market exposure — replacing lost revenue from a shorter regular season. All other teams get a true bye week. 5. All-Star Weekend Before March Madness Hold it the weekend before the NCAA Tournament tips off. Then shut down until the Monday after March Madness’ opening weekend. Players rest, fans recharge, anticipation builds. 6. Playoffs Start Memorial Day Weekend Launch postseason on a national holiday. Big window. Big audience. 7. NBA Finals Wrap Late July No NFL. No college. Just NBA. Take over the summer.

Jokic is the inverse of Rudy Gobert. Jokic is one of the greatest offensive players of this era and maybe of all time. As a Center, he bends the offensive halfcourt with his incredible touch around the rim, his masterful passing and diverse post game. On defense, he is decent in space and but provides minimal rim protection as a Center. Having him on your team guarantees you a Top 10 offense, and limits your defensive ceiling. Rudy Gobert is one of the greatest defensive players of this era and maybe of all time. He is not great in space but provides all time great rim protection. On offense, he has some of the worst hands of any active player, constantly fumbling the ball. He has terrible touch at the rim and is on offense the worst 7 foot post players you will ever see in your life. Having him on your team guarantees you a Top 10 defense and limits your offensive ceiling. Jokic has 3 MVPs Rudy has 4 DPOY Obviously I’m not trying to say these players have similar value. Just an observation of their juxtaposition as elite one way players that have underperformed in the playoffs. With Rudy having his best chance at a Championship this year, it’s possible these two will have had comporable success in this era (strictly based on how their 1 way impact contributes to win %). Is it possible Jokic is overrated, Rudy is underrated or both?

2025 West finals is the first West finals since 1996 where neither team is a California team or a Texas team.. Since 1980, there have been only five West finals where neither team was neither a California team or a Texas team. 1990: Portland Trailblazers defeat Phoenix Suns in 6 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1977. 1992: Portland Trailblazers defeat Utah Jazz in 6. 1993: Phoenix Suns defeat Seattle Supersonics in 6 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1976. 1996: Seattle Supersonics defeat Utah Jazz in 7 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1979. 2025: Oklahoma City vs. Minnesota Timberwolves From 1980-2024, there have been only 9 West champions that were not a California team or a Texas team. 1990, 1992: Portland Trailblazers 1993, 2021: Phoenix Suns 1996: Seattle Supersonics 1997, 1998: Utah Jazz 2012: Oklahoma City Thunder 2023: Denver Nuggets Of these 9 West champions, 2023 Denver Nuggets were the only one to win the NBA Championship.

Commercials (and using replay challenges and fouls/timeouts to slam more of them into the broadcast) have gone too far.. Everyone has noticed that the amount of commercial breaks, and their length, has gone way up. It might be a reaction to lower ratings, but it's gone too far. Between first and third quarters: commercial break, quick check-in, ANOTHER commercial break Replay challenges: this is egregious, since the NBA is already sending refs to the monitor more often to put cameras on their Emirates patches. Each one of these is one, sometimes TWO commercial breaks. Flagrant reviews: see above, each one is more $$$ for the NBA from Emirates and from selling commercials. Fouls: I've noticed players "drawing fouls" right before the scheduled TV timeout, to ensure an extra commercial break without a coach calling timeout. Westbrook did this in Game 6. ESPN halftime is of course an abomination, but it's always been like that and I tune out during that time anyway. I'm more concerned with the constant breaks in gameplay. Are fans just gonna let the NBA become the NFL?

NBA has to get their shit straight with what upgrades to Flagrant. Variation in refereeing on a game by game basis is fine. But when it comes to reviews, there is no room for error, and consistency is required. Part of the reason why there isn’t consistency is because the rules are pretty cloudy. It needs to get straightened out. For the OKC DEN series, in G6 Jdub elbowed Murray in the head, it was reviewed and determined to be a common foul. Today in G7 AG elbowed SGA in the head, and it was upgraded to Flagrant. Very similar plays, the defender’s both entered the other guys space and got hit on pull throughs. I don’t have a problem with it being a flagrant or not, but these two plays can’t result in different calls following a review. It has to go one way or the other. Links below

Nuggets Starters Dominant Playoff Run. Net Rating: +11 (2nd best lineup min 150min) ORTG: 119 DRTG: 108 282 minutes (most out of any playoff lineup out of necessity) So if they were so elite why did they end up in two 7 game series and ultimately losing? Well its because they basically got destroyed anytime that any healthy starter was out. Jokic off -24 Braun off -25 Gordon off -26 Murray off -54 (big reason why he led the league in playoff minutes per game and in total minutes they were getting obliterated when he was off) Every game it was the same script the starters start off well, the first subs come in and they start losing, the starters try to close the game while not gassing out. Today was a great example of this once again happening when the Nuggets were up 11 until Russell Westbrook checked in following Peyton Watson shortly after and the Thunder immediately cut it to 5. Hopefully this kills the Jokic superman +/- narrative since Jokic had a -24 net rating when he played without Jamal Murray. This is going to be the 2nd out of the last 3 playoff runs that Jokic is a negative when he plays without Murray.(-11 in 22/23) This doesn’t mean that Jokic is dependent on Jamal but basketball is a team game and every player plays a part. The whole 1 player carrying concept has always been asinine. Jokic and Murray together without Gordon were a -19 so 2 players carrying isn’t possible either. Depth matters and like we just saw for the Nuggets 5 players isn’t enough to win a championship.

How to win in the playoffs - Guards who play with energy - OKC has 3 guards with more than 0.5 Steals per 36 and over 3 fouls per game - Dort, Caruso and Wallace. 11 Teams had at least one guard - all 8 made the 2nd round.. NBA Playoffs is all about defense this year. Especially these hustle and energy guards who can crash the boards, play aggressive defensive that is hands-on and not worry about fouling, and as a plus, rebound and get offensive rebounds. From [NBA.com]( for players with MP > 100, 0.5 steals per game, 3 fouls per game.  The teams that are not in this list - LA Lakers, Houston Rockets, Bucks, Magic and Heat. Who can forget seeing Alex Caruso body-up 3-Time MVP Nikola Jokic with so much success? These guards are so valuable. They need to be able to play with full intensity and not worry about fouling. The more guards you have, the higher chance of making it past the 1st round. If I was an NBA GM, I would try to acquire these guards onto your team to build a championship contending roster. The proof is there. Guards who are willing to play aggressive defense, able to take good fouls, get deflections steals and rebounds, they are going to take you far into the playoffs. Congrats to OKC with 3 of these players. Personal Fouls is extremely important. The Refs adapt to the physicality of the game. If you are playing super aggressive defense, the refs will start to not make as many calls since the bar goes up on what is considered a foul. The winner of Indiana and Knicks will be Mathurin vs. Josh Hart. Whoever wins this matchup will win the series.

He had a Hall of Fame resume before playing a single minute in NBA. Luka was **expected** to establish himself as an MVP-level player, which happened. Only a handful of rings should count.... If the goalpost keeps getting moved like it does currently. A ring is "only one ring" now. Outside of IDK, Hakeem rings, the pistons in 2004 and the Mavs in 2011 no other ring should count because every star player had either a stacked team or beat "weak"/injured teams in the finals. Bird had McHale and Parish. Kareem and Magic had each other and a couple more hall of famers. MJ had Pippen, Rodman and a couple other hall of famers Duncan had Ginobili, Robinson, Parker and Kawhi. Shaq and Kobe had each other. Toronto beat the injured Warrios and so on. Every single title run can have an asterisk put onto them. Enjoy when your team wins and don't be petty when it loses. Also don't be petty when it wins, that's even worse.

I think with both teams reliance on wings that are above average but also inconsistent shooters, there is going to be some brutally low-scoring games. I'm curious as to if this style of defense going to be increasingly prevalent across the league during the next few seasons? It seems as though a lot of organizations on the rise over the last 2 years (OKC, MIN and ESPECIALLY HOU/IME UDOKA) have realized the success you can have with this. If I'm in a front office watching these series with goals of post season success, I'm going to start holding power slap matches at my workouts (also hiring Metta Sandiford-Artest and Bill Laimber as assistant coach, just in case). On the other side of the playoffs in the East, we have the return of a classic 90s playoffs matchup, the Pacers and Knicks. This will be a little bit different from their distant encounters with both teams offensively oriented during the regular season (Knicks OTRG 4th and Pacers 9th) but both have stepped it up defensively during the post season. During the regular season the knicks and pacers DTRG were both 113.3, good for 13th and 14th respectively. In the playoffs, the Knicks are currently up to 110.6 and the pacers are at 111.9 with a clear increase in intensity. Overall, I'm looking forward to the comparisons of Ant to MJ, the references to the "choke" move of Reggie Miller, and the footage of Spike Lee that rivals that of Taylor Swift at a Chief's game.

The Denver Nuggets have extraordinarily limited options to retool this offseason amongst all eliminated teams.. Set aside whether or not one views Michael Porter Jr. and his contract as a slight overpay or an albatross: The Denver Nuggets have so few assets they can attach to him or their other overpaid player in Zeke Nnaji. While they do own their 1sts in 2026, 2028, 2030-32, due to the Stepien Rule they can't trade their even-numbered picks as they owe first-round draft capital to the Thunder in 2027 & 2029. In essence, their pool of assets that wouldn't be considered untouchable for a non-star trade i.e. you aren't trading Christian Braun, is one pick in either 2031 & 2032, pick swaps for their even-numbered picks up to 2030 and the rookie DaRon Holmes who has yet to play a single game in the NBA. And no, they don't have any second round picks aside from the recently accessible 2032 2nd round pick.

SGA has made a conference finals and potentially gotten MVP before Joel Embiid and Donovan Mitchell have made a single conference final. Gilgeous-Alexander finished the regular season as the first Canadian to lead the NBA in scoring and the third international player to achieve the feat. He finished the season with the most 20-point games (75), 30-point games (49), 40-point games (13) and 50-point games (4). His streak of 72 consecutive games scoring at least 20 points became the longest such streak in NBA history, eclipsing Michael Jordan’s previous record. It was also Gilgeous-Alexander’s third consecutive season averaging at least 30 points, joining a list occupied by Michael Jordan, Wilt Chamberlain, Oscar Robertson, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, James Harden and Bob McAdoo. He has now made a conference finals and probably has won MVP

With the Nuggets loss, the NBA will officially have 7 different champions in 7 years. It is the first time in NBA history where 7 different teams have won the title in a 7 year span. The previous record was 6 different champions in a 6 year span.. Dating back to 2019, the last 6 NBA championships have been won by 6 different team. With the Nuggets, Warriors, and Celtics losing in the second round, the 2025 championship will be won by a different team as well. For the first time in NBA history, there will be 7 different champions in a 7 year span: - 2019 Champion: Raptors - 2020 Champion: Lakers - 2021 Champion: Bucks - 2022 Champion: Warriors - 2023 Champion: Nuggets - 2024 Champion: Celtics - 2025 Champion: It will be the Pacers, Knicks, Timberwolves, or Thunder [Looking through all the other champions in NBA history]( there is only one instance that came close to this (6 different champions in 6 years): - 1975 Champion: Warriors - 1976 Champion: Celtics - 1977 Champion: Blazers - 1978 Champion: Bullets - 1979 Champion: Supersonics - 1980 Champion: Lakers It appears that the NBA has successfully achieved its goal of eliminating dynasties. By the metric of championships, this is the most parity that the league has had in its history.
Do you think it is good or bad that different teams will win the NBA Championship seven years in a row?
